summary: Living in Mecklenburg County, North Carolina, I've seen firsthand the struggles many face with hunger and social services. Even after transitioning from commodities to the food stamp program, as of July 1, the last nineteen counties, including ours, were mandated to participate in food stamps. This place is home to many who need support, and my work has focused on addressing these local issues.

Charlotte has always been a complex city with various competing groups and interests. I was involved here for many years, witnessing how organizations like the Chamber of Commerce and Central Piedmont Industries influenced local politics and business. The city has evolved, especially since the late '50s and early '60s, when being Chamber president was more powerful than being mayor. I also ran for mayor here in 1961 and faced significant opposition because of my stance on equal opportunity for African Americans.

I was a member of the Chamber of Commerce for many years because I respected their goals, but I felt they never truly recognized executive women like myself. Unlike most men whose companies paid their membership, I paid my own way. The Chamber once held immense power in Charlotte, even more than the mayor in the late '50s and early '60s, but its influence has waned over time, especially with the rise of other groups like Central Piedmont Industries.

Central Piedmont Industries has grown to have more clout in Charlotte than the Chamber of Commerce today. While it doesn't actively recruit candidates for local elections, it supports certain ones who usually win. This industrial organization represents a significant force in local politics and business, shaping decisions behind the scenes.

The Central Charlotte Association, formerly known as the Downtown Merchants Association, has played an important role in uniting downtown merchants and elevating women to their proper recognition. I was honored by them with a silver bowl, which shows their appreciation for my efforts. Though they claim not to participate in politics, their business interests naturally involve political influence, especially concerning urban redevelopment.

Dwight Phillips was a major figure in Charlotte's real estate scene and a self-appointed king-maker who tried to control political candidates through his financial influence. He had humble beginnings as a chauffeur for the original U.S. senator but rose to power mainly through money rather than academic or intellectual credentials. Despite his efforts, I never allowed him to control me politically.

Paul Younts was deeply involved in real estate and was considered the king-maker of the Democratic Party in Charlotte for many years. His influence stretched from before his indictment related to campaign fundraising as postmaster, through his military service which led to a suspended sentence. Younts wielded considerable power in local politics during his time.

Charles Ervin was a significant figure in Charlotte's business and political circles, having left Belk and later selling his business to outsiders. Though he seemed to have influence behind the scenes, especially in relation to Brookshire, he was not very visible publicly. His support was once pivotal in local elections, such as when he shifted allegiance between candidates like Brookshire and Gib Smith.

Brookshire was a key political figure in Charlotte who was supported by influential business leaders like Charles Ervin. During the 1961 mayoral campaign, downtown interests recruited multiple candidates including Brookshire to oppose me because of my advocacy for equal opportunity for African Americans. The political dynamics around Brookshire reflected the tensions in Charlotte's power structure at the time.

Gib Smith ran for office in 1965, and there was uncertainty about whether Charles Ervin would support him or Brookshire. Ervin had promised Brookshire his support but then went into business with Smith, showing the shifting alliances in Charlotte politics. Smith's campaign highlighted the complex relationships among local power players.

Belk was a major retail business in Charlotte where Charles Ervin worked before selling his business interests. The company was part of the downtown commercial landscape that influenced local politics and business decisions. Ervin's departure from Belk marked a shift in his role within the community and political scene.

The Democratic Party in Charlotte was heavily influenced by local power brokers like Paul Younts, who used his position as postmaster to raise funds for the party. The party's dynamics were intertwined with real estate and business interests, and political campaigns often involved behind-the-scenes maneuvering. My own experiences running for office showed how the party and its king-makers could affect election outcomes.

Roosevelt was mentioned in relation to Paul Younts' political activities; Younts campaigned for Roosevelt and was later indicted for requiring postal employees to contribute to the party. This connection shows how national politics intersected with local power structures in Charlotte. Roosevelt's era was a backdrop for some of the political maneuvers I witnessed.
